title: Types of advertising and advertising techniques
paperName: Paper 3 - Specialist Options: Approaches, Issues and Debates
summary: 9990 Consumer — TV, digital, print advertising and persuasive techniques.
learningObjectives: Explain emotional, fear, and humour appeals.
Evaluate effectiveness of different media channels.

simpleExplanation.title: Ads that stick
simpleExplanation.summary: Advertisers match message to medium — from emotional TV stories to targeted social feeds — using techniques that bypass rational evaluation.

flashcards.back: Mass media — TV, radio, cinema, billboards — broad reach, high cost, brand awareness focus.
Targeted promotions — email, samples, sponsorship, POS — direct, measurable, conversion focus.
Evokes feelings (happiness, nostalgia, fear) rather than rational product features — mood-dependent processing.
Highlights negative consequences (e.g. smoking ads) — effective only if solution offered and audience feels able to act.
Transfers admired qualities to product via association — source credibility and attractiveness (2.5.2 ELM peripheral route).
Links product with desirable lifestyle/image (not direct claims) — classical conditioning approach.

sections.question: A car brand runs an emotional TV advert showing a family road trip (ATL). It also sends personalised emails with test-drive offers to website visitors (BTL). A celebrity athlete appears in both. A public health campaign uses fear appeals showing accident statistics, paired with safety feature information. Compare the techniques and evaluate their likely effectiveness.
sections.solution:
**TV family road trip — ATL + emotional appeal:** **Mass reach** builds **brand awareness** (2.5.3) and **positive affect** — associates car with **family bonding**, not specifications. **High-involvement** product (2.3.1) — emotion creates **favourable attitude** before rational comparison.

**Personalised email — BTL:** **Targeted** to interested visitors — higher **conversion rate**, measurable ROI. **Test-drive offer** = **behavioural call to action**.

**Celebrity athlete — endorsement + peripheral route:** Transfers **success, performance, attractiveness** to brand. Processed via **peripheral route** (2.5.2 ELM) — cue-based, not argument-based.

**Fear appeal — public health:** **Statistics** create **anxiety** → effective **only if** safety features offer **clear solution** and audience feels **efficacy** (protection motivation theory). Without solution → **denial/defensive avoidance**.

**Evaluate — ATL vs BTL:** ATL builds **long-term recognition** but hard to measure; BTL drives **immediate action** but limited reach. **Combined** = integrated campaign.

**Evaluate — limitations:** **Emotional ads** — **wear-out effect** with repetition. **Celebrity** — **negative publicity** risk. **Fear appeals** — **ethical** concerns if causing distress without actionable advice. **Digital targeting** — **privacy** concerns.
